title: Santa Teresa, Rio de Janeiro

infobox.value: (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈsɐ̃tɐ teˈɾezɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a neighborhood in the city of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). It is located on top of the Santa Teresa hill, by the centre of Rio, and is famous for its winding, narrow streets which are a favourite spot for artists and tourists.

**Santa Teresa** (Portuguese pronunciation: [[ˈsɐ̃tɐ teˈɾezɐ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Portuguese "Help:IPA/Portuguese")) is a neighborhood in the city of [Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio de Janeiro"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"). It is located on top of the Santa Teresa hill, by the centre of Rio, and is famous for its winding, narrow streets which are a favourite spot for artists and tourists.

The neighborhood originated around the *Santa Teresa Convent*, built in the 1750s on the *Desterro* hill. At the end of the 19th and early 20th century it was an upper class borough, as testified by its many opulent villas. Santa Teresa ceased being an upper-class neighbourhood long ago, but it has been revived as a fashionable hotspot. It is home to several artists and art [studios](/wiki/Studio "Studio") and [galleries](/wiki/Art_gallery "Art gallery"). The offer of restaurants and bars is also varied.

One of Santa Teresa's most illustrious inhabitants was [Raymundo Ottoni de Castro Maia](/w/index.php?title=Raymundo_Ottoni_de_Castro_Maia&action=edit&redlink=1 "Raymundo Ottoni de Castro Maia (page does not exist)") [[pt](https://pt.wikipedia.org/wiki/Raymundo_Ottoni_de_Castro_Maia "pt:Raymundo Ottoni de Castro Maia")], an art collector who lived in his *Chácara do Céu* mansion in the neighborhood. The estate was turned into a museum (*Museu da Chácara do Céu*) and its exhibits include works by [Matisse](/wiki/Matisse "Matisse"), [Jean Metzinger](/wiki/Jean_Metzinger "Jean Metzinger"), [Eliseu Visconti](/wiki/Eliseu_Visconti "Eliseu Visconti"), [Di Cavalcanti](/wiki/Di_Cavalcanti "Di Cavalcanti"), and [Candido Portinari](/wiki/Candido_Portinari "Candido Portinari"). It is located near the cultural center [Parque das Ruínas](/wiki/Parque_das_Ru%C3%ADnas "Parque das Ruínas"). In 2006, some paintings were stolen, and have not yet been recovered.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Another museum is the *Museu do Bonde*, which tells the history of the famous [Santa Teresa Tram](/wiki/Santa_Teresa_Tram "Santa Teresa Tram") since its historic origins. A ride on the tram is a popular attraction among tourists. It sports crossing the old [Carioca Aqueduct](/wiki/Carioca_Aqueduct "Carioca Aqueduct"), going through picturesque streets of the Santa Teresa neighbourhood and wonderful views of the city downhill.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/LeandroJoaquim-1790-Arcos.jpg/250px-LeandroJoaquim-1790-Arcos.jpg)](/wiki/File:LeandroJoaquim-1790-Arcos.jpg)

View of the Convent of *Santa Tereza* (on the hill, to the right) in a 1790 painting. The pond in front was filled for the construction of the [Passeio Público](/wiki/Passeio_P%C3%BAblico_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "Passeio Público (Rio de Janeiro)").

### Santa Teresa Convent

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Santa Teresa Convent")]

The neighborhood arose in the 1750s on the  *Morro Desterro* ("Right Hill"), emerging from the eponymous Catholic [convent](/wiki/Convent "Convent"). It was one of Rio de Janeiro's first expansions out of the initial Portuguese settlement in the city center. Initially inhabited by the upper class of the time, several houses and [mansions](/wiki/Mansions "Mansions") were built, inspired by French architecture of the time, many of which are standing today. All throughout its history, Santa Teresa received many [European immigrants](/wiki/Immigration_to_Brazil "Immigration to Brazil").

Around 1850 many people, fleeing the [yellow fever](/wiki/Yellow_fever "Yellow fever") epidemic in the city, flocked to the neighborhood. As it was at a higher altitude, Santa Teresa was less affected by the epidemic than the surrounding neighborhoods.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Head-on_view%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro_tram_10_on_Rua_Murtinho.jpg/250px-Head-on_view%2C_Rio_de_Janeiro_tram_10_on_Rua_Murtinho.jpg)](/wiki/File:Head-on_view,_Rio_de_Janeiro_tram_10_on_Rua_Murtinho.jpg)

The [Santa Teresa Tram](/wiki/Santa_Teresa_Tram "Santa Teresa Tram") has run over the [Carioca Aqueduct](/wiki/Carioca_Aqueduct "Carioca Aqueduct") since 1896.

### Santa Teresa tram

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Santa Teresa tram")]

Main article: [Santa Teresa Tram](/wiki/Santa_Teresa_Tram "Santa Teresa Tram")

In 1872, a [tram](/wiki/Tram "Tram") line was constructed to connect Santa Teresa to downtown Rio de Janeiro, which further advanced the access to the neighborhood. The tram ran up the streets of *Rua Joaquim Murtinho* (named after [Joaquim Murtinho](/wiki/Joaquim_Murtinho "Joaquim Murtinho")) and *Rua Almirante Alexandrino*, crossing the [Carioca Aqueduct](/wiki/Carioca_Aqueduct "Carioca Aqueduct"). Initially, the tramcars were drawn by [mules](/wiki/Mule "Mule"), before electric tramcars replaced the [mule- or horse-drawn trams](/wiki/Horsecar "Horsecar") in 1896. The tramcars' colours ranged from green to silver and blue, but came to be painted bright yellow after complaints from residents who said that the tram "disappeared" amid the greenery of the neighborhood. The iconic yellow trams – or *bondes* in [Brazilian Portuguese](/wiki/Brazilian_Portuguese "Brazilian Portuguese") – have become a symbol of Santa Teresa.

By means of organized demonstrations, residents were able to preserve the historic tram system by convincing the government to release funds for the streetcars. After an accident with a tram in 2011 that killed five people,[[3]](#cite_note-3) all service on the tram line was suspended for four years, in order to modernize the system.[[4]](#cite_note-derailment-4)[[5]](#cite_note-taut-2015oct-5)[[6]](#cite_note-g1-aug2015-6) Newly built, replica-vintage tramcars in the same style as the old fleet were acquired, and the line was rebuilt and reopened in stages starting in 2015. It was restored to its full length of 6 km (4 mi) in January 2019.[[7]](#cite_note-taut-2020apr-7)

### Revitalization

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Revitalization")]

Over time, Santa Teresa lost its status as an upscale neighborhood, like many of the historic districts of Rio's [South zone](/wiki/South_Zone_(Rio_de_Janeiro) "South Zone (Rio de Janeiro)"). In recent years however, it has become a district of cultural interest because of its architectural value and prime location. In 2009 the city council started a [gentrification](/wiki/Gentrification "Gentrification") program to revitalize the neighborhood. Walls were built to stop the surrounding [favelas](/wiki/Favela "Favela") from growing; and taxes were imposed on water, energy and gas supply in order to decrease the number of poor people.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/Escalera_Seleron.jpeg/250px-Escalera_Seleron.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Escalera_Seleron.jpeg)

[The Selarón stairs](/wiki/Escadaria_Selar%C3%B3n "Escadaria Selarón").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a7/SantaTeresa-Rio.jpg/250px-SantaTeresa-Rio.jpg)](/wiki/File:SantaTeresa-Rio.jpg)

Picturesque corner of Ladeira de Meireles

Traditionally Santa Teresa is a renowned gathering place for [intellectuals](/wiki/Intellectual "Intellectual"), academics, artists and politicians, who are attracted by its historic character, cultural life and standard of living. Due to this profile, it is a former district of opinion with political participation in popular movements. There are also a large number of [NGOs](/wiki/Non-governmental_organization "Non-governmental organization") who provide services and support to the favelas located around Santa Teresa.

Santa Teresa is also a vibrant [gastronomic](/wiki/Gastronomy "Gastronomy") center, mainly around the Largo do Guimarães, as well as an artistic center. Due to the large number of artists and art studios, the neighborhood has come to be called *Rio's [Montmartre](/wiki/Montmartre "Montmartre")*. The [bohemian](/wiki/Bohemianism "Bohemianism") lifestyle has contributed to Santa Teresa being a main tourist attraction of Rio de Janeiro, as well as its recognition as one of the most unique neighborhoods in the world in 2016.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

The neighborhood, affectionately called *"Santa"* by locals, is composed of several steep staircases, slopes, cobbled roads and tortuous alleys, which provide the character of the neighborhood. These sloping roads connect Santa Teresa to the surrounding districts of [Centro](/wiki/Centro,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Centro, Rio de Janeiro"), [Glória](/wiki/Gl%C3%B3ria,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Glória, Rio de Janeiro"), [Laranjeiras](/wiki/Laranjeiras,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Laranjeiras, Rio de Janeiro"), [Fátima](/wiki/Bairro_de_F%C3%A1tima,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Bairro de Fátima, Rio de Janeiro"), [Cosme Velho](/wiki/Cosme_Velho "Cosme Velho"), [Catumbi](/wiki/Catumbi "Catumbi"), [Catete](/wiki/Catete,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Catete, Rio de Janeiro") and [Rio Comprido](/wiki/Rio_Comprido,_Rio_de_Janeiro "Rio Comprido, Rio de Janeiro"). At the highest point of Santa Teresa, which gives access to [Tijuca National Park](/wiki/Tijuca_National_Park "Tijuca National Park") and [Corcovado](/wiki/Corcovado "Corcovado"), there is a broad panoramic view of the city.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Associação Civil de Divulgação Cultural e Educacional Japonesa do Rio de Janeiro](/wiki/Associa%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Civil_de_Divulga%C3%A7%C3%A3o_Cultural_e_Educacional_Japonesa_do_Rio_de_Janeiro "Associação Civil de Divulgação Cultural e Educacional Japonesa do Rio de Janeiro") ("Civil Association of Japanese Educational and Cultural Dissemination of Rio de Janeiro", known in Japanese as the "Rio de Janeiro Japanese School"), a [Japanese international day school](/wiki/Nihonjin_gakko "Nihonjin gakko"), was previously located in Santa Teresa.[[9]](#cite_note-9) It is now located in [Cosme Velho](/wiki/Cosme_Velho "Cosme Velho").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Panorama

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Santa_Teresa,_Rio_de_Janeiro&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Panorama")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/1_santa_teresa_panorama_2014.jpg/960px-1_santa_teresa_panorama_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:1_santa_teresa_panorama_2014.jpg)

Aerial panorama from Santa Teresa: [Botafogo neighborhood](/wiki/Botafogo "Botafogo") left, [Sugarloaf Mountain](/wiki/Sugarloaf_Mountain "Sugarloaf Mountain") in the back and [Christ the Redeemer](/wiki/Christ_the_Redeemer_(statue) "Christ the Redeemer (statue)") on top of [Corcovado](/wiki/Corcovado "Corcovado") in the top right corner.
